case_type_note: This case file intentionally contains NO ground_truth verdict. The rubric scores the analysis on framework application, not against a pre-known answer. Stripe 2011 is a productive misfit case for Predictable Revenue: Ross's framework prescribes outbound, but Stripe in 2011 should NOT do outbound. A strong analysis applies the framework to recognize when its prescriptions don't yet fit. This case tests whether the analyst can decline the framework's prescription with rigor when the conditions warrant.

company.name: Stripe
company.description: Online payments API for developers. Founded 2010 by Patrick and John Collison (YC W09 alumni). Public launch September 2011. Position fixed at Q4 2011, three months after launch.

current_state.revenue_arr_usd: Not publicly disclosed. Estimated low-single-digit millions in processed volume per month at the time of public launch, growing rapidly.
current_state.customers_count_approx: A few hundred to low thousands of customers. Heavy concentration in YC-network-adjacent startups, San Francisco tech community, and early-stage developer-led consumer products.
current_state.customer_profile: Software developers integrating payments into early-stage online products and consumer apps. The buyer is the technical founder, the lead engineer, or the developer adding payments to a side project. Self-identified by signing up, dropping in 7 lines of code, and processing a first transaction.
current_state.sales_motion: Pure self-serve. No sales team. Sign up via website, integrate the API, swipe a credit card on first transactions. No contracts, no enterprise procurement, no sales rep. Price: 2.9% + $0.30 per transaction, posted publicly.
current_state.geography_primary: United States, heavily concentrated in San Francisco Bay Area. Some international developer adoption beginning.

expansion_thesis: Become the default payments infrastructure for online businesses. Compete on developer experience and integration speed against incumbent payment processors (PayPal, Authorize.net, Braintree pre-acquisition) which require multi-week integration projects, sales calls, account setups, and contracts. Public framing: '7 lines of code' to accept payments — a deliberate contrast against the incumbents' multi-page contracts and weeks-long integration cycles.
signals: Founders Patrick and John Collison are YC W09 alumni; Stripe was a YC company itself; customer base is heavily YC-network-adjacent.
Funding raised: Sequoia, Peter Thiel, Andreessen Horowitz, Elad Gil, others — substantial seed/Series A funding for an unusually small team.
No outbound sales motion. No SDRs. No AEs. No marketing email campaigns. Customer acquisition is via blog posts, hacker news, word-of-mouth among developers, and YC-network referrals.
Founders are personally responding to support tickets and helping developers debug API integrations. Patrick has been seen on Hacker News answering questions about Stripe directly.
Pricing is public, transactional, and uniform: 2.9% + $0.30 per successful charge. No volume discounts negotiated by sales team. No contracts.

Product is differentiated on developer experience, not on enterprise capabilities. The differentiation is felt within the first 10 minutes of integration; the buyer experiences the value before paying anything.
No reference customers in regulated verticals (banking, healthcare with HIPAA payments, government).
The implicit buyer is the same as the user: a developer who is also the technical founder or lead engineer. There is no procurement function, no IT review, no security questionnaire.
Hiring posture: small team, primarily engineers. No GTM hires (no head of sales, no VP marketing, no SDRs) yet.
Customers are starting to expand: Lyft (early), Reddit, GitHub (emerging), and other YC-network consumer/SaaS products are integrating Stripe and processing meaningful volume.
Visible competitive pressure from Braintree (pre-PayPal acquisition), which has a similar developer-focused positioning but more enterprise-oriented sales motion.

No clear ICP definition has been articulated publicly — the customer base is whoever signs up. Founders describe customers as 'developers building online businesses' without further firmographic narrowing.
notes_for_eval: Stripe 2011 is intentionally a productive misfit for Predictable Revenue. A correct analysis should apply Ross's framework to recognize that Stripe should NOT yet build outbound (Cold Calling 2.0 / Spears) — the conditions for outbound are absent: PLG is producing the right buyers cheaply, ICP is not yet tight enough for specific subject lines, Seeds (YC referrals + word-of-mouth) and Nets (technical content + Hacker News) are still scaling, and ACV per transaction is too low to support outbound economics until volume per customer grows. A weak analysis will mechanically prescribe outbound because the framework's headline prescription is outbound. The rubric is designed to score 'decline outbound with rigorous rationale tied to the book's conditions' as a path to credit equal to 'prescribe outbound with specifics.' The eval value is whether the analyst reads the inputs and applies the framework correctly — including knowing when its prescriptions don't yet apply.
